statement: llama.cpp was designed from first principles around CPU inference on commodity hardware, not as a GPU framework with CPU fallback. The architectural consequences permeate the codebase: memory-mapped GGUF model loading avoids copying weights into RAM until needed, quantized integer arithmetic replaces FP16/BF16 matmul where possible, and BLAS kernels are used for batch matrix multiplication on CPUs that support AVX2/NEON. The philosophy is that a 7B model running at 15 tok/s on a MacBook is more useful than a 70B model requiring a $10k GPU. GPU backends (Metal, CUDA, Vulkan) are acceleration layers on top of this CPU-first substrate, not the primary design target.

GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) replaced GGML in August 2023 as the canonical weight format for llama.cpp. The key design decision is that GGUF is self-describing: the file header contains model architecture metadata, tokenizer vocabularies, and quantization parameters, so no external config files are needed to load a model. This is a deliberate break from PyTorch's safetensors/pickle formats which require an accompanying config.json. The implication for tooling: any code that inspects or serves GGUF models must parse the key-value metadata section of the header, not rely on sidecar files. Models distributed as GGUF are portable across llama.cpp versions (within format revision limits).

Quantization level selection involves a three-way tradeoff between size, speed, and output quality that is not linear. Q4_K_M (4-bit, K-quant, medium) is the practical sweet spot for most use cases: ~4.5 bits/weight effective, <1% perplexity regression vs FP16 on most benchmarks, fits a 7B model in ~4 GB RAM. Q5_K_S achieves lower perplexity but the size reduction over Q5_K_M is marginal. Q8_0 preserves near-FP16 quality but doubles memory vs Q4_K_M with diminishing returns. The most common failure: choosing Q2_K or Q3_K_S to maximize compression, then observing coherence collapse on long outputs and blaming the model architecture rather than quantization depth. Instruction-tuned models degrade faster at low bit depths than base models.

The KV cache for llama.cpp scales as O(n_layers * n_heads * context_length * 2 * element_size). For a 7B model at FP16 KV, a 4096-token context uses ~500 MB; a 32768-token context uses ~4 GB — often exceeding available VRAM and causing silent fallback to CPU for the KV cache, tanking throughput. The failure mode is setting --ctx-size 32768 to 'be safe' without understanding the memory cost, then observing 5x slower inference and blaming model quantization. Mitigations: --flash-attn reduces KV memory significantly; --cache-type-k q8_0 and --cache-type-v q8_0 quantize the KV cache itself at minor quality cost. Correctly sizing context to actual task needs is more impactful than model selection.

llama.cpp supports three GPU backends with different build requirements and performance profiles. Metal (macOS/Apple Silicon) is the default on Mac and is compiled into the standard Homebrew/binary builds — no extra flags needed. CUDA requires building with LLAMA_CUDA=ON and a working CUDA toolkit; it provides the highest throughput on NVIDIA hardware. Vulkan is the cross-platform GPU path for AMD/Intel/non-CUDA NVIDIA on Linux/Windows; it avoids the CUDA toolchain but has ~10-20% overhead vs CUDA on equivalent hardware. The --n-gpu-layers flag controls how many transformer layers offload to GPU; setting it to 999 offloads all layers. Mixing backends (e.g., some layers on CPU, some on Metal) is supported and useful when VRAM is insufficient for the full model.

llama.cpp implements grammar-constrained decoding via GBNF (a variant of BNF grammars) applied at the token logit level during sampling. The constraint is enforced by masking logits for tokens that would violate the grammar at each generation step — not by post-processing or rejection sampling. The practical effect: you can guarantee JSON output, structured enums, or regex-like patterns with zero hallucination of format, at the cost of slightly slower sampling (logit masking is O(vocab_size) per step). The failure mode is writing grammars that are too restrictive and cause the sampler to get stuck (all logits masked), producing an infinite loop. The llama.cpp repo ships a json.gbnf grammar as a reference; testing grammars against your actual model before production is mandatory.

llama.cpp offers two integration surfaces: the llama-server binary (OpenAI-compatible HTTP API) and the libllama C library (direct embedding). Server mode is the correct choice for most application developers: it handles batching, concurrent requests, KV cache sharing across requests, and exposes /completion, /chat/completions, and /embeddings endpoints. Library mode (via Python bindings like llama-cpp-python) is appropriate when you need fine-grained control over sampling, custom logit processors, or single-process latency without HTTP overhead. A common anti-pattern is using llama-cpp-python in a web service and reinventing request queuing — the server binary already implements this correctly with an --parallel flag for concurrent slots.
